items.evidence_text:
Client: It’s around, uh, 120k a year.
Advisor: Okay, about 120k gross. Is that salary plus everything, or just your base?
Client: That’s basically my total. I think it’s actually 123,300 gross for the year if I’m being exact.
Advisor: Great—$123,300 gross annually. Let me note that down. And you’re the only adult in the household, right—just you?
Client: Yeah, one adult. It’s just me.

Advisor: Perfect, that clears it up. Switching to expenses—what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in? Ballpark is fine.
Client: Like 7 grand a month. Maybe a little less.
Advisor: Okay, around $7,000. Does $6,800 a month sound closer, or is that too low?
Client: No, $6,800 is pretty much it. That’s the number I’ve been using.
Advisor: Great—$6,800 per month total expenses. Now debt payments—do you have a mortgage or any other monthly debt costs?

Advisor: Okay, about $2,100. And just to make sure we’re not mixing up balance versus payment—$2,100 is the monthly payment, not what you owe total, right?
Client: Oh—yeah, sorry, monthly payment. The balance is way bigger than that.
Advisor: Totally normal mix-up. So monthly mortgage cost—would you say it’s $2,150 a month?
Client: Yeah, $2,150 is the actual payment.
Advisor: Great. And the interest rate on that mortgage—do you know it offhand?

Advisor: Okay, about $275,600 for the home. Now I want to pivot to investable assets—everything that’s not the house. Roughly how much do you have invested across accounts?
Client: I mean, I’d say like 350k-ish? Maybe 360.
Advisor: Okay, mid-300s. If you had to pin it down, is it closer to $367,300 total investable assets?
Client: Yeah—actually, yes. $367,300 is the total I saw when I pulled it together.
Advisor: Great. Let’s break that down so we’re not hand-waving. Do you have a larger taxable account—what’s the biggest bucket?

Client: 2031. June 2031, I think.
Advisor: Perfect—June 2031. And the home value—what would you put your primary residence at today?
Client: Around 275,600. That’s close to what Zillow said last time I checked.
Advisor: Okay, about $275,600 for the home. Now I want to pivot to investable assets—everything that’s not the house. Roughly how much do you have invested across accounts?
Client: I mean, I’d say like 350k-ish? Maybe 360.

Client: I think it’s 5.1%.
Advisor: 5.1%, got it. And roughly what’s the outstanding balance still owed?
Client: About 104,400 left.
Advisor: Okay—$104,400 outstanding. Do you remember when it’s scheduled to be paid off—like the final payment year?
Client: 2031. June 2031, I think.

Client: I think it’s 5.1%.
Advisor: 5.1%, got it. And roughly what’s the outstanding balance still owed?
Client: About 104,400 left.
Advisor: Okay—$104,400 outstanding. Do you remember when it’s scheduled to be paid off—like the final payment year?
Client: 2031. June 2031, I think.

Client: Stability, honestly. Like I’m fine taking risk, but I don’t want to blow up what I’ve built after the divorce.
Advisor: Got it—growth, but with guardrails. Going back to that stability point, cash flow is usually the stressor. Can we start with income—what’s your gross household income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around, uh, 120k a year.
Advisor: Okay, about 120k gross. Is that salary plus everything, or just your base?
Client: That’s basically my total. I think it’s actually 123,300 gross for the year if I’m being exact.

Advisor: Alright, Michael—going back to what you said earlier about wanting stability for the kids, I want to make sure the household basics are right before we talk investments.
Client: Yeah, okay.
Advisor: First one’s simple: marital status—are you currently divorced, married, single, what should we put down?
Client: Divorced. Been that way a bit now.
Advisor: Got it—divorced. And in the household itself, is it just you as the adult in the home, or is there another adult living with you?

Client: Stability, honestly. Like I’m fine taking risk, but I don’t want to blow up what I’ve built after the divorce.
Advisor: Got it—growth, but with guardrails. Going back to that stability point, cash flow is usually the stressor. Can we start with income—what’s your gross household income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around, uh, 120k a year.
Advisor: Okay, about 120k gross. Is that salary plus everything, or just your base?
Client: That’s basically my total. I think it’s actually 123,300 gross for the year if I’m being exact.

Client: It’s around, uh, 120k a year.
Advisor: Okay, about 120k gross. Is that salary plus everything, or just your base?
Client: That’s basically my total. I think it’s actually 123,300 gross for the year if I’m being exact.
Advisor: Great—$123,300 gross annually. Let me note that down. And you’re the only adult in the household, right—just you?
Client: Yeah, one adult. It’s just me.

Advisor: Okay—32-ish. When you say “bracket,” are you thinking federal marginal bracket, not your effective rate after deductions?
Client: Uh… I mean what comes out of my paycheck? Like the withholding.
Advisor: Yep, that’s the common mix-up. Withholding is just what gets sent in during the year; bracket is the marginal federal rate on the next dollar of income. Does 35% sound more like where you land, or still 32?
Client: Hang on—yeah, you’re right. My CPA said 35%. So put me at thirty-five percent.
Advisor: Great—35% tax bracket. Thanks for correcting that.

Client: Sure.
Advisor: Alright. Going back to what you said earlier about wanting upside but also not losing what you’ve built—Michael, how would you describe your risk comfort if I had to put it in one bucket?
Client: I’m pretty aggressive, honestly. I can handle swings, I just don’t want to do anything stupid with the kids depending on me.
Advisor: Aggressive—got it. And when you say aggressive, you mean you’re okay with volatility in the market, not like taking on a bunch of new debt, right?
Client: Yeah—market volatility. Not debt. I’m not trying to rack up loans.

Advisor: Totally fine. So residence state: Other. And country is the US—correct?
Client: Correct, US.
Advisor: Great. Now, small misunderstanding I see a lot—when I say “market,” I don’t mean the stock market. I mean the type of advisory channel you’re working with. Are you working with a US RIA setup, like a registered investment advisor here in the States?
Client: Wait—market like… Nasdaq? Sorry.
Advisor: Exactly—different “market.” This is more like the platform/channel. US RIA versus, say, a bank brokerage or something overseas.

Advisor: Split how—like some paid quarterly and some annually?
Client: Exactly. One chunk hits quarterly—about 29,600 a year when you annualize it.
Advisor: Okay, quarterly-paid interest/dividends, annualized about $29,600. And the other chunk is paid annually?
Client: Yeah, the other one is annual, and that’s about 93,700 a year.
Advisor: Got it—annual-paid interest/dividends, about $93,700 annualized. Hang on—when you say “paid annually,” you mean you receive it once a year, not that it’s your yearly total from that account?

Advisor: Perfect. We’ll keep it tight. Before we get into numbers, tell me a little about what you want this plan to do for you and the kids.
Client: Stability, honestly. Like I’m fine taking risk, but I don’t want to blow up what I’ve built after the divorce.
Advisor: Got it—growth, but with guardrails. Going back to that stability point, cash flow is usually the stressor. Can we start with income—what’s your gross household income these days, roughly?

Advisor: Alright—going back to what you said earlier about keeping it quick, I hear you. I just need to lock down a couple of basics about you, Michael, and then we’re done with the admin stuff.
Client: Okay. Yeah, let’s do it.
Advisor: First one: are you currently working—like W-2 employed—or are you self-employed right now?
Client: Employed. I’m still at the same company.
Advisor: Got it. And just so I’m not mixing gross and take-home—when we say your annual income, are we talking gross pay before taxes and deductions?

Client: Yeah, sales.
Advisor: Okay. Next: date of birth. What’s your birthday, Michael?
Client: May 21st, 1989.
Advisor: May 21, 1989—thank you. I always double-check because people swap month/day on me.
Client: Yeah, no, it’s May.

Advisor: Okay—$104,400 outstanding. Do you remember when it’s scheduled to be paid off—like the final payment year?
Client: 2031. June 2031, I think.
Advisor: Perfect—June 2031. And the home value—what would you put your primary residence at today?
Client: Around 275,600. That’s close to what Zillow said last time I checked.

Client: Yeah, $275,600 is fine.
Advisor: Cool. Quick tangent—did you have to fight traffic getting back from work today, or are you remote?
Client: Traffic. Sales calls all over, so it’s kind of nonstop.
Advisor: Yeah, that’ll do it. Alright—back to it.
Advisor: So on the liabilities side, I’ve got one mortgage at 5.1% with a $2,150 monthly payment, final payment June 2031—
